coredata.dc:description: The highly efficient red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and benzil via a facile solvothermal process. The R-CDs displayed maximum fluorescence properties at excitation and emission wavelengths of 550 and 631 nm, respectively, which fall within the red wavelength range. Moreover, the R-CDs exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield of 11.3 %, and this fluorescence was effectively quenched by Sunset Yellow (SY). Consequently, a novel fluorescent probe was developed for SY detection. This probe exhibited a linear range of 0.085–11.31 μg/mL and limit of detection of 0.026 μg/mL. The R-CDs were validated for SY quantification in various food samples, including carbonate beverages, powdered beverage, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples, achieving recovery rates of 91.2–122 % and a relative standard deviation of 1.0–3.5 %. The synthesized R-CDs therefore show promise for application as a probe for the detection of SY in foods.

head.ce:title: Red-emissive carbon dot as fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of sunset yellow in foodstuffs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities, Xingyi 562400, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly efficient red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and benzil via a facile solvothermal process. The R-CDs displayed maximum fluorescence properties at excitation and emission wavelengths of 550 and 631 nm, respectively, which fall within the red wavelength range. Moreover, the R-CDs exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield of 11.3 %, and this fluorescence was effectively quenched by Sunset Yellow (SY). Consequently, a novel fluorescent probe was developed for SY detection. This probe exhibited a linear range of 0.085–11.31 μg/mL and limit of detection of 0.026 μg/mL. The R-CDs were validated for SY quantification in various food samples, including carbonate beverages, powdered beverage, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples, achieving recovery rates of 91.2–122 % and a relative standard deviation of 1.0–3.5 %. The synthesized R-CDs therefore show promise for application as a probe for the detection of SY in foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sunset Yellow (SY), which is also known as Food Yellow 3, is a monoazo dye that is highly soluble in water, resulting in its widespread use as a composite food colorant (Gong et al., 2023). SY is prized for its strong coloring ability, excellent color stability, and low cost. In addition, due to its versatility, it is used in a variety of food products (Tabanlıgil Calam & Taşkın Çakıcı, 2023), including, soft drinks (Yuan et al., 2016), fruit juices (Ahmadi et al., 2023), beverages (Kaur et al., 2021), sodas, and savory snacks (Cai et al., 2019). However, research has shown that an excessive intake of SY may contribute to health issues such as allergies, anxiety, migraine, diarrhea, asthma, liver damage, kidney damage, eczema, and possibly cancer. These effects have been largely attributed to its aromatic and azo components, which can have toxic and harmful effects on human organs (Kaya et al., 2021; Keshava et al., 2023; Monisha et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023; Wang, Wang, et al., 2022). Based on this category also include benzidine, which has been linked to bladder cancer in humans, so dye workers exposed to azo dyes have a higher rate of bladder cancer. Therefore, azo dyes are lethal, genotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic to both humans and animals (Ding et al., 2019). Consequently, the stringent regulation of SY levels in food products is essential, with the World Health Organization setting a recommended daily intake limit of 4 mg/kg (Bisgin, 2018). It is therefore imperative to prioritize the development of a straightforward, economic, and reliable analytical method for quantifying SY in food matrices.

Although a range of detection techniques are available, including colorimetry (Jia et al., 2019), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Xu et al., 2022), filter paper-based colorimetric analysis (Wang, Zhang, et al., 2022), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Pirvu et al., 2020), electrochemical methods (Vatandost et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2018), electrochemiluminescence (Liu et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2023), and fluorescence-based methods (Huang & Cheng, 2017; Korah & Mathew, 2023; Pan et al., 2023; Su et al., 2021; Zhang, Wang, et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024). However, these methods can be time-consuming and costly. Thus, it is a need to develop a novel, highly efficient, and sensitive methodology for SY quantification. Among the available analytical techniques, the fluorescent probe approach has exceptional selectivity, in addition to being rapid and environmentally friendly. In the context of this fluorescence detection technique, carbon dot (CD) probes have drawn significant attention. CDs were serendipitously discovered Xu in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004) a novel class of luminescent nanocarbon materials, and since then, these materials have been extensively studied (Sun et al., 2006). Based on the superior chemical stability, remarkable biocompatibility, and low costs associated with CDs, they have been widely employed in fluorescence spectroscopy (Korah & Mathew, 2023), biological imaging (Jian et al., 2024), catalysis (Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2024), human health caring (Mansuriya & Altintas, 2021; Tiwari et al., 2023), and anti-counterfeiting measures. Unfortunately, numerous researchers encountered much obstacles in the early stages of CDs research, finding methods like electrochemical exfoliation (Yu et al., 2024) and pulsed laser (Jin et al., 2024) are complex and time-consuming. As a result, the solvothermal synthesis approach has emerged for the preparation of CDs, offering benefits in terms of environmental friendliness, waste minimization, and procedural simplicity. Currently, the majority of CDs employed for the detection of specific components in foods emit blue fluorescence, and it is relatively rare for CDs to display excitation and emission wavelengths in the red wavelength region. Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) differ from blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) in key areas: 1) Emission: R-CDs emit at longer wavelengths, aiding in detecting substances with less scattering; 2) Photostability: R-CDs maintain fluorescence better under prolonged exposure; 3) Spectral properties: R-CDs support multiplex detection for simultaneous target analysis; 4) Biocompatibility: R-CDs offer improved biocompatibility and reduced toxicity; 5) Targeting: R-CDs enhance detection of specific molecules through surface modifications. These benefits highlight R-CDs' potential in food analysis and biological imaging. Suitable reaction solvents and precursors are crucial in synthesizing R-CDs (Cao et al., 2024; Jia et al., 2019; Kirbas Cilingir et al., 2024; Wang, Zhang, et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). These insights have advanced the development of CDs and R-CDs, encouraging research and innovation in this area. Thus, considering the issues associated with excess SY consumption, it would be desirable to prepare R-CDs that can detect SY in food samples.

In this study, innovative R-CDs were synthesized from citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and benzil (1,2-diphenylethane-1,2-dione) via a simple and environmentally friendly solvothermal technique. Remarkably, these R-CDs demonstrated a fluorescence quantum yield of 11.3 %. The exceptional performance of these R-CDs has contributed to the establishment of a fluorescence detection method for detecting trace SY in carbonate beverages, powdered beverage, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples, yielding impressive results (see Scheme 1 ). Additionally, the fluorescence probe method was extended to detect SY in real samples.

Benzil, L-malate, l-ascorbic acid, polyamide, tartrazine, polyethyleneimines, NH4F, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2EDTA), and N-(2-hydroxyethyl) piperazine-N′-(2-ethanesulfonic acid) (HEPES) were acquired via Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). ZnSO4, NaCl, KNO3, KH2PO4, AgNO3, BaCl2, CuSO4, Fe2(SO4)3, NaNO2, NaOH, citric acid, benzoic acid, dextrose, rhodamine B, and ammonia solution (25 wt%) were procured by Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, and L-cysteine were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sodium cyclamate, sorbic acid, and SY were provided by Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, methyl alcohol, and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). HCl, and formic acid were sourced from Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). All experiments utilized ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) to ensure experimental accuracy. Methanol of HPLC grade and ammonium acetate of guarantee reagent were supplied by Tianjing Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All other reagents employed in the study was of analytical grade.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was carried out using a Bruker Dimension Icon microscope (Karlsruhe, Germany). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured with a Hitachi H-8100 TEM (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was recorded using a Perkin Elmer Platinum Spectrum 400 series spectrometer (Shanghai, China). The fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs was tested with an FLS1000 fluorescence and phosphorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Scotland, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The fluorescence data were acquired with a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). To determine the reference SY content, high-performance liquid chromatography was performed using an Agilent 1220 system equipped with a variable wavelength detector (HPLC-UV) (Agilent Scientific, USA).

A solvothermal approach was employed to prepare desired R-CDs. More specifically, 0.478 g of benzil, 0.200 g of polyethyleneimines, along with 0.450 g of citric acid were combined with 60 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Soon afterwards, the mixture was sonicated for 15 min, transferred to a 100-mL vessel, and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particle impurities. The supernatant was then collected to obtain the final R-CD solution for further use.

To inspect the selectivity of the prepared R-CDs, a solution of R-CDs (1.90 mL,18.0 mg/mL) was mixed with a sequence of metal ions and small molecules (100 μL, 100 μM solutions, SY, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, F−, Fe3+, Cl−, K+, Mg2+, PO4 2−, benzoic acid, HEPES, tartrazine, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, dextrose, and l-ascorbic acid). The fluorescence intensity was measured at an emission wavelength of 631 nm and 550-nm excitation immediately after the addition of the above species. Each fluorescence intensity measurement was performed in triplicate (n = 3).

The effect of interference on the probe was also evaluated. Initially, 200 μM solutions of the various metal ions and small molecules (50.0 μL) were mixed with the R-CD solution (1.90 mL, 18.0 mg/mL). Subsequently, the SY solution (200 μM, 50.0 μL) was added to mix with the small molecules and ions system, including Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, dextrose, l-ascorbic acid, citric acid, L-malate, tartrazine, L-cysteine, sorbic acid, sodium cyclamate, Na2EDTA, and NaNO2, respectively. Whereafter, the fluorescence intensities of the system mixed with the potential interferences mentioned above were measured at an excitation wavelength of 550 nm and an emission wavelength of 631 nm (n = 3).

The feasibility and practicality of using the synthesized R-CDs for SY detection in actual samples was validated by testing various food items. The carbonate beverages, powdered beverage, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples were purchased from a local hypermarket. The liquid (4.00 g) and solid (1.00 g) samples were mixed with water (20.0 mL), and the pH of each sample solution was adjusted to 6 using citric acid (0.20 g/mL). After heating to 60 °C, polyamide powder (0.400 g) and a small amount of water were added to create a paste, which was stirred for a specific duration. Subsequently, the paste was washed (∼5 times) with a mixture of ultrapure water, methanol, and formic acid (pH 4) at 60 °C, the polyamide powder was then washed until a neutral pH was obtained. To completely desorb the pigment, the mixture was treated with a combination of anhydrous ethanol, ammonia solution, and water. Then, the residue was dissolved in a specified volume of water, to give a final volume of 2.00 mL. This solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane for prior to analysis (Ou et al., 2018).

The concentration of SY in each food specimen was also measured by HPLC-UV, using a Dikma Diamonsil (2) C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm i.d.; 5 μm particle size; Beijing, China). These mobile phases consisted of a mixture of methanol and water containing ammonium acetate (0.010 mol/L). The proportion of mobile methanol in the phase was gradually adjusted from 30 to 70 % (v/v) between 0 and 8.00 min. The flow rate was maintained at 0.900 mL/min, the injection volume was 15.00 μL, accompanied by the column temperature was 35 °C, and detection wavelength was 254 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the red-emissive carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon sources significantly influence the optical properties of CDs. For R-CDs, precursors typically include (1) heteroatoms that create new energy bandgaps and (2) suitable aromatic structures that enhance sp2 domains, which are essential for tuning emission and excitation to longer wavelengths (Wang et al., 2018). Thus, citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and benzil were utilized as carbon sources. The choice of solvent also plays a crucial role in the dehydration and carbonization of precursors during R-CD synthesis. The size of sp2-conjugated extensions in R-CDs can be adjusted by selecting different solvents in solvothermal synthesis (Wang et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2018). To investigate the effects of various solvents on R-CD synthesis, multiple solvothermal methods were employed. Specifically, 0.478 g of benzil, 0.200 g of polyethyleneimine, and 0.450 g of citric acid were dissolved in 60 mL of solvents including anhydrous ethanol, THF, DMF, acetone, methyl alcohol, and water. The excitation and emission profiles for the different solvents are summarized in Table S1 and Fig. S1. THF and DMF exhibit excitation at 470 nm, outside the red-emission range, whereas water peaks at 500 nm, which is less effective than anhydrous ethanol. Methyl alcohol and acetone demonstrate similar fluorescence to anhydrous ethanol but with lower intensities. Therefore, using anhydrous ethanol for R-CD synthesis is advantageous for measurements.

The crystal morphology of the synthesized R-CDs was analyzed using TEM and AFM. More specifically, the TEM images demonstrated in Fig. 1A clearly reveal a uniform particle size and shape for the R-CDs with negligible aggregation. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy revealed ordered lattice stripe patterns in the central portion of these R-CDs, with an example illustrated in Fig. 1B. The lattice fringes observed, with a period of 0.21 nm, correspond to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite‑carbon (Boukhvalov et al., 2024). AFM characterization, as shown in Fig. 1C, confirmed a uniform distribution of particle heights, indicating a lack of aggregation. Additionally, AFM imaging demonstrated that the surface of the bare R-CDs is relatively smooth, with an average roughness (Rq) of 1.16 nm, suggesting a flat configuration with minimal roughness (Guo et al., 2017).

The R-CDs were characterized by XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy, providing insights into their morphology, surface bonds, and elemental composition. As shown in Fig. 1D, the XRD characteristics display the crystal structures of the R-CDs, confirming that they are composed of amorphous carbon phases. This observation can be attributed to the graphitization of carbon atoms and the presence of abundant functional bonds on the surface of R-CDs. Fig. S2 faultlessly demonstrated that this FT-IR spectrum displays characteristic absorption bands for OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3336 cm−1 (Tan et al., 2021). The peak at 2970 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CH group (Liu et al., 2019). Additionally, a broad peak at 1049 cm−1 can be associated with the C–O–C bond (Liao et al., 2018). Moreover, the peak around 880 cm−1 is belongs to the flexural vibration of the N − H bond (Zhan et al., 2019). Therefore, the presence of above functional groups would be expected to enhance the water solubility of the R-CDs, which should be advantageous for various applications.

Subsequently, XPS was perform to examine the surface composition of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the XPS results revealed the presence of carbon (66.87 %), nitrogen (1.28 %), and oxygen (31.85 %). The reduced nitrogen content in R-CDs compared to precursors arises from synthesis factors, including loss of nitrogen groups during thermal processes and further decreases during R-CD formation, as evidenced by binding energy peaks at 284.2, 400.2, and 532.2 eV. In the C1s spectra of the R-CDs (Fig. 2B), four main peaks corresponding to the C − O, C − C/C=C, C − N, and CO bonds were deconvoluted at 283.7, 284.7, 285.5, and 286.6 eV, respectively (Kong et al., 2020; Kou et al., 2020; Wang, Yue, et al., 2019), while in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2C), peak corresponding to CO, C − OH, and C − O bonds were deconvoluted at 531.2, 532.4, and 532.9 eV (Liu et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2020), respectively. Additionally, in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), the presence of amine N and C − N bonds were confirmed based on the peaks at binding energies of 399.7 and 400.7 eV (Liu et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2020), respectively. These observations suggest that the synthesized R-CDs should exhibit a notable hydrophilicity, consistent with the FT-IR data. Such hydrophilicity would be desirable during sample analysis to promote the application of the R-CDs in biomolecule and ion detection.

The approximate ranges of the characteristic excitation, emission, and absorption peaks for the prepared R-CDs were determined using three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy, as presented in Fig. 3A. Consequently, it was deduced that the excitation range was 440–680 nm, while the emission range was 560–720 nm. It is worth noting that the R-CDs displayed their peak fluorescence at 631 nm when excited at 550 nm, which correspond to the typical excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs. Excitingly, the UV–vis spectrum of the R-CDs demonstrated in Fig. 3B reveals a pronounced absorption band at 270–430 nm, suggesting the presence of π–π* and n–π* transitions, which are associated with the presence of sp2 hybridized CO and CC bonds on the R-CDs' surface, respectively (Hirose et al., 2002; Ji et al., 2022; Yoo et al., 2021). Noticeably, a bright red fluorescence image is clearly visible to the naked eye in the inset of Fig. 3B, produced by irradiating the R-CD solution with a deuterium lamp. These results further suggested that the R-CDs could potentially demonstrate an exceptional water solubility, even without surface passivation.

The stability characteristics of the R-CDs were evaluated by monitoring the effects of the ion concentration and the time on the observed fluorescence at room temperature. As illustrated in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs remained virtually constant over 12 h, indicating minimal variation over an extended period, and highlighting the remarkable stability of the R-CDs. This property was favorable in terms of facilitating subsequent experiments and investigations. Furthermore, the data presented in Fig. 4B show that the overall fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs remained constant even with an increase in the NaCl concentration. Although this result indicates the exceptional ionic resistance of these R-CDs, further exploration using other ions would be beneficial to evaluate their wider ionic resistance. The fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs was then determined via the time-correlated single-photon counting (Fig. 4C), and the decay trajectory was modeled according to Eq. (1).

Further investigation into the optical properties of the R-CDs revealed significant changes in the fluorescence intensity with variation in pH. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity increased from pH 2.0 to 3.0, and then sharply decreased beyond pH 4.0. This result suggests partial aggregation of the R-CDs, since this structural change results in a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity owing to the decreased net surface charge of the R-CDs. Notably, the pH of the prepared R-CD solution ranged from 4.0 to 5.0, wherein the fluorescence intensity is particularly high, thereby favoring ion detection under these conditions. Considering the favorable optical stability of the prepared R-CDs, their suitability for use as a nanoprobe in practical applications was evaluated. More specifically, in the presence of rhodamine B, the quantum yield of the R-CDs was calculated to be 11.3 % (see Fig. S3).

To evaluate the selectivity of the R-CDs toward SY detection, a variety of small molecules and ions that are found in common foods were tested (i.e., SY, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, F−, Fe3+, Cl−, K+, Mg2+, PO4 2−, benzoic acid, HEPES, tartrazine, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, dextrose, and l-ascorbic acid). Upon mixing these species with a solution of the prepared R-CDs, the fluorescence intensity was promptly measured under 550 nm excitation (n = 3), and the change in fluorescence (ΔF) was calculated using the formula ΔF = F 0 − F, where F 0 is the fluorescence of the 18.0 mg/mL R-CD solution, and F is the fluorescence of the ion-bound complex (Yuan et al., 2016). As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the R-CDs exhibit a distinct response to SY. Indeed, in the presence of the above potential interferents at the same concentration level as SY (see Fig. 5B), no significant interference was observed in SY sensing, confirming the exceptional anti-interference ability of the developed R-CDs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5C, the emission intensity of the R-CDs decreased with an increasing SY concentration, demonstrating effective fluorescence quenching of the R-CDs by SY over a concentration range of 0–11.31 μg/mL. Moreover, the calibration curve presented in Fig. 5D reveals a linear range of 0.085–11.31 μg/mL (0.75–100 μM) for SY measurement, with a coefficient of determination (R
2 ) of 0.998. The limit of detection was determined to be 0.026 μg/mL (0.23 μM) based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Additionally, Table S2 details the comparison of this proposed method for SY with other detection methods (Bao et al., 2022; Malik et al., 2022; Medeiros et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018). It offers advantages such as simple preparation, low cost, a wide linear range, remarkable performance, and a low detection limit. These results suggest that the R-CD probe has potential for use in the analysis of various sample types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sunset yellow in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the excellent selectivity and sensitivity of the as-prepared R-CDs toward SY, the developed method was applied to quantification of SY in carbonate beverages, powdered beverage, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples. The detection conditions, including excitation and emission wavelengths of 550 and 631 nm, respectively, are matchlessly detailed in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . The concentrations of SY in the samples were found to be 1.67 μg/g for the carbonated beverage, 1.69 μg/g for the powdered beverage, 0.430 μg/g for cider vinegar, 2.19 μg/g for the fruit flavored drinks, 1.11 μg/g for chocolate, 0.216 μg/g for hard candy. The satisfactory recovery for these real samples ranged from 91.2 % to 122 %, along with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.0 % to 3.5 % (n = 5). These results peerlessly demonstrate the excellent feasibility, rapidity, and precision of the established fluorescence method. Soon afterwards, the HPLC-UV was then employed for method comparison and validation. As shown in Fig. 7A, a linear detection range was defined between 0.0500 and 100 μM (R
2 = 0.9998), which was matchlessly suitable for accurately determination of SY content in these aforementioned samples. These results in Figs. 7B-F incredibly demonstrated that HPLC-UV was applied to further substantiate these satisfying results were in accordance with measured results, which confirmed the accuracy of the established R-CDs way and highlighting its potential application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism toward sunset yellow detection
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism involving R-CDs and SY can be elucidated through thorough research and meticulous analysis. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of SY and the excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs were examined. The 100 μM SY absorption spectrum exhibited a peak in the 200–600 nm range, overlapping with the excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs + 100 μM SY, as circumstantiated in Fig. 8A. The excitation and emission spectra of the R-CDs overlap greatly with the absorption spectrum of SY elaborated in Fig. 8B. This phenomenon suggests that the SY-induced quenching mechanism may involve either an inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Guo & Cai, 2020; Wen et al., 2015).

To further elucidate the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs in the absence and presence of SY were tested. As demonstrated in Fig. 8C, the average lifetimes for R-CDs and R-CDs + 100 μM SY were 4.74 ns and 4.78 ns, respectively, with a marginal difference of only 0.04 ns. The similarity in fluorescence decay patterns suggests a plausible internal IFE, indicating that the SY-induced quenching is static, ruling out FRET. Thus, the fluorescence quenching may be attributed to IFE.

Molecular interactions (MIs) can also contribute to the quenching of fluorophores (Malik et al., 2015; Rong et al., 2014). In our synthesis process, the fluorescence quenching of R-CDs may result from hydrogen bonding interactions between the −OH groups on R-CDs and the hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring of electron-deficient SY (Zhang et al., 2018). Additionally, an apparent blue shift in the emission peak occurs with increasing SY concentration, as illustrated in Fig. 8D, further confirming the existence of MIs between the R-CDs and SY. Thus, SY-induced fluorescence quenching of R-CDs is attributed to both IFE and MIs. While IFE can be a source of error in fluorometry, it has recently been utilized in novel fluorescent assays.

In summary, highly efficient red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid, polyethyleneimine, and benzil via a facile solvothermal process. These R-CDs were found to exhibit an excellent fluorescence effect and a high fluorescence quantum yield. In addition, the prepared R-CDs demonstrated an excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of the SY dye, which was not affected by the presence of various interferents. Consequently, a platform was established for the detection of SY in carbonated beverages, powdered beverages, cider vinegar, fruit flavored drinks, chocolate, and hard candy samples. The obtained results demonstrated that these synthetic R-CDs have potential for application in the detection of undesirable SY in foodstuffs, thereby contributing to improved food safety and quality control.
